{{Coord|34.477861|N|135.773944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara, Nara]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kashihara|WD=Q496319}}| region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of [[:en:Mount Unebi|Mount Unebi]] in the southern part of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as [[:en:Roman Empire|Roman]] glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the [[:en:Western Regions|Western Regions]] via [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the late 5th century. In 2014, [[:en:X-ray fluorescence|X-ray fluorescence]] analysis at the [[:en:Tokyo University of Science|Tokyo University of Science]] revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of [[:en:Roman glass|Roman glass]] excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] and are collectively designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kashiharajingū-mae Station|Kashiharajingū-mae Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Kashihara Line|Kashihara Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of [[:en:Mount Unebi|Mount Unebi]] in the southern part of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, [[:en:Doshisha University|Doshisha University]] and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as [[:en:Roman Empire|Roman]] glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the [[:en:Western Regions|Western Regions]] via [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the late 5th century. In 2014, [[:en:X-ray fluorescence|X-ray fluorescence]] analysis at the [[:en:Tokyo University of Science|Tokyo University of Science]] revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of [[:en:Roman glass|Roman glass]] excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] and are collectively designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from [[:en:Kashiharajingū-mae Station|Kashiharajingū-mae Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Kashihara Line|Kashihara Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{ill|Kashihara|WD=Q496319}}| region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group]][[en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:新沢千塚古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group]][[en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:新沢千塚古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group]][[en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:新沢千塚古墳群]]
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster | native_name =新沢千塚古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group, gaikan-kita-1.jpg; File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group, gaikan-kita-1.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|34.477861|N|135.773944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location ={{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}; {{ill|Kashihara|WD=Q496319}}| region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group]][[en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:新沢千塚古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group]][[en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:新沢千塚古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group]][[en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:新沢千塚古墳群]]
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster | native_name =新沢千塚古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group, gaikan-kita-1.jpg; File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group, gaikan-kita-1.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|34.477861|N|135.773944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location ={{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}; {{ill|Kashihara|WD=Q496319}}| region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (park) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|40.3|N|135|46|26.2|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-48, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.48 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-81, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.81 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-109, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.109 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-115, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.115 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-126, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.126 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-137, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.137 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-139, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.139 新沢千塚126号墳出土 金製方形板・金製垂飾付耳飾.JPG|Gold square plate and gold pendant for ear ornaments unearthed from Kofun No.126 新沢千塚126号墳出土 玉類.JPG|Jade unearthed from Kofun No.126 新沢千塚126号墳 出土品-2.JPG|Excavated items from Kofun No.126 </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi|1=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman|1=Roman Empire}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions|1=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence|1=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science|1=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass|1=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station|1=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line|1=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi|1=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman|1=Roman Empire}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions|1=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence|1=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science|1=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass|1=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station|1=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line|1=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-48, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.48 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-81, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.81 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-109, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.109 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-115, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.115 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-126, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.126 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-137, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.137 File:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Group-139, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.139 新沢千塚126号墳出土 金製方形板・金製垂飾付耳飾.JPG|Gold square plate and gold pendant for ear ornaments unearthed from Kofun No.126 新沢千塚126号墳出土 玉類.JPG|Jade unearthed from Kofun No.126 新沢千塚126号墳 出土品-2.JPG|Excavated items from Kofun No.126 </gallery>

{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi|1=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman|1=Roman Empire}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions|1=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence|1=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science|1=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass|1=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station|1=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line|1=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}} is a group of over 600 [[Kofun period]] [[kofun| Kofun]], located in the Kita-Ochi and Kawanishi neighborhoods of the city of {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1972 .<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210850|title=屋敷山古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> Its also known as the {{nihongo|'''Toriya Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|鳥屋千塚古墳群|}} or the {{nihongo|'''Kawanishi Senzuka Kofun Cluster'''|新沢千塚古墳群|}}
==Overview== The Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster in the northwest of the Ochioka Hills, on the hills that can be seen on both the north and south sides of the Toge-Kume Prefectural Road. The site stretches two kilometers east-to-west and two kilometers north-to-south to the south of {{ill|Mount Unebi|en|Mount Unebi|WD=Q17606448|lt=Mount Unebi|1=Mount Unebi}} in the southern part of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. It consists of more than 600 Kofuns, most of which are {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, measuring 10 to 30 meters in diameter, but the site also includes nine {{nihongo|''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=[[Zenpō-kōen-fun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, one {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles, and twelve {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} rectangular tumuli. These Kofuns were built from the end of the 4th century to the 7th century, and were particularly active from the mid-5th century to the end of the 6th century. The majority of the internal facilities are wooden coffins for direct burial, but some have clay coffins, gravel coffins, and horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
[[Archaeological excavation]]s began in 1947, and a full-scale survey was carried out in 1962 . At this time, 23 tumuli were excavated, including Kofun No. 500 (a keyhole-shaped tomb) with a clay coffin. In Kofun No. 500, {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} similar to those from the early Kofun period were found, including a rare {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} known as a hanging mirror. This led to the site being designated as a National Historic Site. In the 1960s, {{ill|Doshisha University|en|Doshisha University|WD=Q921088|lt=Doshisha University|1=Doshisha University}} and other organizations conducted an investigation, investigating about 130 tumuli. A wealth of grave goods, including weapons and horse equipment, were excavated, but in Kofun No. 126, which appeared to be a small rectangular Kofun from the outside, a large number of grave goods made of gold, silver, glass, and jade including a lacquer plate, a copper handled dish, a mirror, a bead, a square gold plate with dragon pattern arabesque openwork, a silver hollow bead, a hanging gold earring, a glass dish, a glass jar, gold rings and belt fittings were excavated. The artifacts also included a fire iron (a metal vessel used as an iron by putting charcoal inside), which was the first excavated in Japan, as well as {{ill|Roman Empire|en|Roman Empire|WD=Q2277|lt=Roman|1=Roman Empire}} glass products that are thought to have been brought by trade from the {{ill|Western Regions|en|Western Regions|WD=Q1190423|lt=Western Regions|1=Western Regions}} via {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} in the late 5th century. In 2014, {{ill|X-ray fluorescence|en|X-ray fluorescence|WD=Q898974|lt=X-ray fluorescence|1=X-ray fluorescence}} analysis at the {{ill|Tokyo University of Science|en|Tokyo University of Science|WD=Q1069994|lt=Tokyo University of Science|1=Tokyo University of Science}} revealed that the chemical composition of a glass dish excavated was almost identical to that of {{ill|Roman glass|en|Roman glass|WD=Q1138592|lt=Roman glass|1=Roman glass}} excavated within the Roman Empire.<ref name="Shiseki"/> The artifacts from Kofun No. 126 are kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} and are collectively designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/481021|title=火熨斗|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
The site is preserved as a park and is about 25 minutes on foot from {{ill|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|en|Kashiharajingū-mae Station|WD=Q6374283|lt=Kashiharajingū-mae Station|1=Kashiharajingū-mae Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Kashihara Line|en|Kashihara Line|WD=Q2491066|lt=Kashihara Line|1=Kashihara Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
